issue_title: more crispness around `Variable` vs `Tensor`
issue_body:
a lot of our methods are type-hinted to take `torch.Tensor` but we're really giving them `torch.autograd.Variable`s.  

in particular, this is biting me in `PytorchSeq2VecWrapper` which says that its mask is a Tensor, but it's not, and if you try to find the number of non-zero elements with

```
        num_valid = torch.sum(mask[:, 0])
```

you get another variable and you have to call `.data` on it, whereas you wouldn't if mask was really a Tensor.

(it's a nuisance that mypy doesn't pick up on this, but :man_shrugging: )

anyway, I can deal with this by doing 

```
        if isinstance(num_valid, torch.autograd.Variable):
            num_valid = num_valid.data[0]
```

but that's kind of gross. I'd like to come up with a more "global" approach for handling this and also get our type hints reflective of what we're actually doing.

issue_title: Tests rely on corpus files
issue_body:
The corpus is licensed differently than the rest of music21. See music21/corpus/license.txt. 
In instances where a user decides to only install the BSD/LGPL part of music21, it would be helpful to have minimal dependencies against the corpus. In general there are quite a few dependencies:
> grep corpus music21/*/*py | wc -l
980
 
Most of these can likely be avoided. Many tests rely on corpus files, for example testTonalAmbiguityA in music21/key.py. It would be helpful if (a) tests were not in the same files as code being tested [but that's perhaps a personal choice] :-) and more importantly (b) tests for BSD/LGPL code didn't depend on data running under a different license.  I suspect most or all tests can be run against test data explicitly designed to exercise the code being tested. 

Thanks!

issue_title: Design Document: Provisioning Deis
issue_body: Design Document: Provisioning Deis ================================== Provisioning Deis is currently an adventure; usually a treacherous adventure. We've fielded many provisioning related problems on our #deis irc channel, many of which amounted to small missteps in the [manual process](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/installing_deis/install-platform/). We as the Deis maintainers have been frustrated with the provisioning process during our normal development workflows and especially during release testing time. See: #4318 (thanks @krancour for starting this public discussion!) and #2882. We've been dreaming of a future where provisioning and deploying Deis to any cloud provider is a trivial and an elegant experience. Design Goals ------------ * Make the provisioning process less painful, more seamless, and faster for all users * Provide a way to manage and update long-living Deis clusters * Allow anyone to run our full suite of tests on any cluster * Create a common user experience for provisioning Deis regardless of what provider is used * Avoid implementing yet another desired state infrastructure tool (Terraform) * Construct a buffer between users and the details of Deis' installation process and internal architecture * Bridge the gap between basic and advanced deployments of Deis * Enable users to service multiple Deis clusters simultaneously Proposal: `rigger` ---------------- In order to achieve our dream of a more elegant Deis provisioning process for us as maintainers and everyone as users, we'd like to build a new tool specific to Deis called: `rigger`. Why the name? A rigger is a person who specializes in the lifting and moving of extremely large or heavy objects. They understand the ins and outs of static and dynamic loads and their expertise is of paramount importance to ensure that the components of a project are delivered safely and expediently into the exact positions. Consider the complexities involved for a rigger when [replacing a 38 ton thruster on the DCV Balder](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Bti0Z5a7GmE). Therefore, naming this tool "rigger" makes sense: it's your highly experienced aide in lifting your Deis platform into position. `rigger` is your interface to deploying Deis on a variety of cloud providers. `rigger` will: * ask questions from the user up front instead of requiring full attention during the whole provisioning processes * abstract the details of cloud providers and Deis provisioning quirks by default but allow you to coordinate details if you have specific demands * show you how it created the infrastructure (an audit log and package of important components of the provisioning process will be provided to users) * be held within a separate git repository from the core Deis project. * this will enable us to release updates to the provisioning process outside of the core Deis release cycle. Our plan is to keep a [strict interface ](#cloud-provider-interface) between `rigger` and the cloud providers already written in Deis' contrib directory. If we do have a need to break this interface, we can build in the smarts about which interface `rigger` should use per version of Deis. So what will this look like for you? For a **contributor**, hacking on Deis becomes: rigger configure # asked a set of questions rigger checkout # checks out Deis repo rigger provision # provisions Deis cluster from checked out repository ... [![asciicast](https://asciinema.org/a/25505.png)](https://asciinema.org/a/25505) For a **tester**, testing Deis becomes: rigger configure # asked a set of questions (including what version you'd like to test) rigger provision rigger test # can choose what type of test to run or run manual tests at this point [![asciicast](https://asciinema.org/a/25506.png)](https://asciinema.org/a/25506) For an **operator**, provisioning Deis becomes: rigger configure rigger provision rigger test rigger save rigger configure --file rigger upgrade --to rigger configure --file rigger provision # could be modifications to change infrastructure below Deis `rigger`'s user interface ---------------- ### `rigger configure` ##### Inputs The command will ask the absolute minimum amount of questions needed to provision a Deis cluster. Such as: 1. What version of Deis? [latest Deis release] 2. [What cloud provider would you like to use? [Digital Ocean]](http://deis.io/code-for-credit-digitalocean/) 3. What type of cluster are you looking to create? [development] 4. What are your cloud provider credentials? [suggestion] ##### Operation If no file is passed in, `rigger` will: - ask necessary questions to fill in a complete `rigger` config file - consider the file to be the "current" environment (used for future `rigger` commands) If a file is passed in, `rigger` will: - validate the file - ask any questions to fill in missing information in the file - consider the file to be the "current" environment (used for future `rigger` commands) ##### Outputs a configuration file This file is used as the source of truth for any future `rigger` operation. It could also be sourced into a shell environment to allow more advanced users to easily dive into the inner workings of a Deis cluster (config file could hold DEIS_VERSION, PROVIDER, GOPATH, DEIS_ROOT, DEIS_TEST_DOMAIN) export DEISCTL_UNITS="/Users/username/.rerun-deis/490572dde4/units" export DEIS_ROOT="/Users/username/go/src/github.com/deis/deis" export DEIS_TEST_AUTH_KEY="/Users/username/.ssh/deis-test" export DEIS_TEST_DOMAIN="deis.xyz" export DEIS_TEST_ID="490572dde4" export DEIS_TEST_ROOT="/Users/username/.rerun-deis/490572dde4" export DEIS_TEST_SSH_KEY="/Users/username/.ssh/deis-test" export DEIS_VARS_FILE="/Users/username/.rerun-deis/490572dde4/vars" export DEV_REGISTRY="registry.hub.docker.com" export GOPATH="/Users/username/go" export IMAGE_PREFIX="username/" export ORIGINAL_PATH="/Users/username/.rbenv/bin:/Users/username/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin" export PATH="/Users/username/go/bin:/Users/username/.rbenv/bin:/Users/username/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin" export PROVIDER="aws" export VERSION="1.9.1" ### `rigger checkout` ##### Inputs ##### Operation Ability to clone, just update, or leave the target alone ##### Outputs DEIS_ROOT contains the version of Deis that you'd like to use (determined in the configure stage) ******************************************************************************** ### `rigger provision` ##### Inputs ##### Operation Use config file contents to: - load provider logic from deis.git (see expected provider interface) - create a cluster of machines via cloud provider - install and start Deis Could also use this on an existing cluster to increase/decrease/configure infrastructure ##### Outputs 1. a working Deis cluster! 2. configuration file updates... The cluster provisioning process will produce information necessary to interact with the Deis cluster in future operations, such as DEISCTL_TUNNEL: export DEISCTL_TUNNEL="ec2-54-201-171-20.us-west-2.compute.amazonaws.com" export DEISCTL_UNITS="/Users/username/.rerun-deis/490572dde4/units" export DEIS_PROFILE="test-490572dde4" export DEIS_ROOT="/Users/username/go/src/github.com/deis/deis" export DEIS_TEST_AUTH_KEY="/Users/username/.ssh/deis-test" export DEIS_TEST_DOMAIN="test-490572dde4.deis.xyz" export DEIS_TEST_ID="490572dde4" export DEIS_TEST_ROOT="/Users/username/.rerun-deis/490572dde4" export DEIS_TEST_SSH_KEY="/Users/username/.ssh/deis-test" export DEIS_VARS_FILE="/Users/username/.rerun-deis/490572dde4/vars" export DEV_REGISTRY="registry.hub.docker.com" export ELB_DNS_NAME="deis-test-DeisWebE-1WG53EPRDVT11-1617219712.us-west-2.elb.amazonaws.com" export ELB_NAME="deis-test-DeisWebE-1WG53EPRDVT11" export GOPATH="/Users/username/go" export IMAGE_PREFIX="sgoings/" export ORIGINAL_PATH="/Users/username/.rbenv/shims:/Users/username/.rbenv/bin:/Users/username/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin" export PATH="/Users/username/.rerun-deis/490572dde4/bin:/Users/username/go/bin:/Users/username/.rbenv/shims:/Users/username/.rbenv/bin:/Users/username/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin" export PROVIDER="aws" export STACK_NAME="deis-test-490572dde4" export STACK_TAG="test-490572dde4" export VERSION="1.9.1"``` ******************************************************************************** ### `rigger test` ##### Inputs type of test to run - full - smoke - specific component - app deploy + test (deploy some apps that we can keep an eye on during the upgrade process) - app test (make sure previously deployed apps are responding correctly) - app test + destroy (make sure previously deployed apps are responding correctly, and then remove them) ##### Outputs Summary of tests run and their results ******************************************************************************** ### `rigger destroy` ##### Inputs ##### Outputs completely destroyed cluster that was referred to in the configuration file ******************************************************************************** ### `rigger setup-clients` ##### Inputs ##### Outputs deisctl and deis clients (including appropriate units files) installed in a directory available to the user which could be added to the user's PATH ******************************************************************************** ### `rigger save` ##### Inputs ##### Outputs package containing all relevant configuration, manipulation, and information of what's been done to a certain environment... such as: - audit log - Terraform state files - complete userdata files - parameters passed into various scripts - `rigger` config file - deis test results ******************************************************************************** ### `rigger upgrade` ##### Inputs --to supply the version that you want to upgrade your Deis cluster to --upgrade-style [graceful, inplace] graceful: (no application downtime) use the new (as of 1.9.0) Deis upgrade logic inplace: (more downtime) use the documented way of tearing down the whole cluster and reinstalling Deis ##### Operation Verification that the upgrade is worth it (an upgrade from 1.9.0 -> 1.9.0 doesn't make sense) ##### Outputs Your old cluster, but with the version of Deis you specified! `rigger`'s interface for cloud providers ---------------- Below are our requirements for building a set of scripts that `rigger` expects to exist. Each operation (installation of deps, creating a cluster) will just call the appropriate shell script. How you implement the logic of creating a cluster is left up to the developer (as long as the dependencies are installed via the *Install Dependencies* script)! ##### Install Dependencies contrib//install.sh ##### Create Cluster contrib//create.sh ##### Check Cluster contrib//check.sh ##### Destroy Cluster contrib//destroy.sh Future Ideas ============ - make a web service on top of `rigger` that people could use to provision a Deis cluster

issue_title: Task pause-info has inconsistent exit codes
issue_body:
Take an unpaused task, e.g. a completed task.

`globus task pause-info TID -Ftext` will exit 1.
`globus task pause-info TID -Fjson` will exit 0.

Either both should exit 0 or both exit 1.

issue_title: Restrict broken pipe handling to IO locations
issue_body:
Right now, all of execution is wrapped in a handler for broken pipes designed to handle the possibility that python IO is being truncated by a consumer like `head`.
This is somewhat dangerous, and it would be better to wrap the IO sites with this handling.

This can be a context manager, but a special purpose `globus_cli.safeio.write(..., file=sys.stdout)` might be desirable.

Needs a bit more thought, and some investigation of `click.echo` -- maybe that does what we want, and just needs to be wrapped in our own package so that we can slot something there later if we replace Click.

issue_title: mkprof command doesn't write empty tables
issue_body:
The `mkprof` subcommand of the `delphin` command is meant to replicate [art](http://sweaglesw.org/linguistics/libtsdb/art)'s behavior, but currently empty table are not written. The `--full` option could be used for copying non-empty profiles, but there's no way to write the empty tables like `art` does.

Proposal: add a `--skeleton` option for the current behavior (only `item` and `relations` are written), and the default behavior writes all tables define in Relations, even if empty.

issue_title: Proxy configuration per URL
issue_body:
In a complex network with multiple proxies, requests need to be routed to different proxies that depend on the URL.  I propose that we extend the proxy_servers key in the conda config to support arbitrary prefixes, rather than just 'http' and 'https' keys, and that the longest matching key should be used.

For example, the proxy_servers config could be:

```yaml
proxy_servers:
    http: http://some.proxy.com:8080
    https: https://some.proxy.com:8081
    'http://some.internal.url': http://internal.proxy.com
    'http://some.external.url': http://external.proxy.com
```
Any request is matched to the longest string, and that given proxy is used.

A weakness of this proposal is that often you want to match the end of a domain name in proxy configurations, i.e., any domain that ends in 'mycompany.com' should use a specific proxy.  However, for the specific purposes of conda, perhaps it's all right to hardcode the specific url conda would use, like 'http://mypackages.mycompany.com'.

This addresses the problems in #190.

issue_title: idxstats in python3 returns a byte object instead of a string
issue_body:
I am using pysam version 0.9.0 and python3.4.

When running idxstats I get a bytes object instead of an string. Python 2 returns a string.

```
>>> idxstat = pysam.idxstats(bam_file)
>>> type(idxstat)
<class 'bytes'>
```

This is problematic because if one tries to split `idxstats` and error occurs:

```
>>> idxstat.split("\n")
TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'str'
```

The solution is to use `decode()` to convert the bytes object into a `str` but I think that pysam should return a string by default. 

This problem could be related to pysam opening a file as binary (see http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33054527/python-3-5-typeerror-a-bytes-like-object-is-required-not-str)

issue_title: Simplifying connect() function when not using .eapi.conf
issue_body:
I am using pyeapi in a context where I want arguments like username/password to come from the Ansible inventory (potentially from Ansible's vault) and not from .eapi.conf.

I look into the code a bit and was messaging with Ryan Madsen of Arista. connect_to function looks to be hard-coded to use the arguments from the .eapi.conf file. I then tried to just use the connect() method.

eapi_conn = pyeapi.connect(transport='https', host='1.1.1.1', username='admin', password='passw', port=8443)

After I did this, I would get the following message when I tried to execute my Ansible playbook. 

AttributeError: 'HttpsEapiConnection' object has no attribute 'enable'

In other words, I expected that I would have the same methods available to me as I had with the connect_to() call. I looked at what you did with connect_to() call and I could see that you were creating a Node() instance.

Ryan then pointed out I could do the following:
>>> cnxn = pyeapi.client.connect(transport="https", host="lf240", username="admin", password="foo")
>>> node = pyeapi.client.Node(cnxn)

I think it would be better if you did one of the following:

1. Have the connect() method return a Node object. I don't know if there are uses of connect() without creating a Node object (i.e. whether doing this breaks backwards compatibility). If backwards compatibility is an issue, then you could add a function parameter like 'return_node=False' and have it default to False so that the behavior only changes if someone calls it with the new argument).

or

2. Update the package __init__.py file and __all__ list to add the Node class. This would make it clearer that you potentially intended users to directly use this class (and then add a message to the docstring for the connect() function on how connect can be used with the Node class).

Instead, it places an empty message in the queue.

issue_title: Output format of ACL hard to work with
azure-cosmosdb-tables does not support CosmosDB Tables Service
issue_body:
This is a case of a unfortunately named library that may lead you to believe you could use it to connect to the CosmosDB Table API. 

Do not be misled by the links on the CosmosDB Table API page: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/table-introduction 

A rename of the project is certainly in order.

I hope that a future version will support the new API soon.

Thank you to @srinathnarayanan for confirming this with me.

issue_title: Street names capitalized incorrectly
issue_body:
This is really a minor issue, but we noticed that all components of street names get capitalized during dataset processing. For example: 

 Bertel Jungin tie 1, Helsinki  -> Bertel Jungin Tie 1, Helsinki.  

In above, 'tie' means a 'road'.  The local convention is to write only proper nouns with a capital letter, whereas common nouns are written with a lowercase initial.

issue_title: Faster (than) git clone
issue_body:
Hi,

I was wondering how could `pre-commit install` execution time could be improved.
Especially, I was considering the [`git clone` command employed](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/master/pre_commit/store.py#L119) to retrieve pre-commit hooks repos.
Hence a few questions:

- is there any specific reason to fetch the whole repo ? A necessity to have its full commit history fetched ?

- could it be considered to only retrieve a single commit ?
Using for exemple one of the 2 methods mentioned here: http://stackoverflow.com/a/26957305/636849

- could we add a heuristic that retrieve archives from Github repos ?
cf. https://developer.github.com/v3/repos/contents/#get-archive-link
```
https://github.com/User/repo/tarball/$sha
https://github.com/User/repo/zipball/$sha
```

- maybe advise hooks repo owner to `git bundle create` their repos to make cloning faster ?

I'm not git specialist, but I some improvment could be done without much cost (simply adding `--depth 1` to the `git clone` could be a start).

issue_title: Allow to execute Python hooks outside a virtualenv
issue_body:
Hi.

I developped a new hook to check Python dependencies against [safety-db](//github.com/pyupio/safety-db):
https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks-safety

It works perfectly well but only if I remove the `VIRTUAL_ENV` environment variable in `languages.python.py:get_env_patch` :
https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/d5ebea3/pre_commit/languages/python.py#L28

Would you be open to a PR that makes it possible to NOT run a Python hook in a virtualenv, based on a `hooks.yaml` config entry ?

issue_title: Kinematic friction broken after recent changes to getOrInitSolverBody.
issue_body: Friction is no longer applied when a dynamic rigid body is resting on a moving kinematic body. I believe that this is caused by the recent change to `btSequentialImpulseConstraintSolver::getOrInitSolverBody`. Note that this is broken with or without `BT_THREADSAFE` set. Using the old version of the function fixes the problem, although this is obviously not threadsafe.

issue_title: CancelledError are not handled correctly even with aiojobs
issue_body:
## Long story short

We are running a internet mobile app which has around 100,000 users accessing each day . Probably，the web server received 3,000 hits per second. 
Since all the user access should be realtime mode . We use aiohttp framework to handle all of them.
Every sec, the error log records around 5,6 cancellederrors which confused us very much . We can not figure out the real cause why so many cancellederrors are threw . 
After several rounds of discussion in GitHub , we got to know the version of aiohttp 2.3.0 has resolved this common problem by using aiojobs to reduce the number of cancellederror occured.
But after 2.3.0 implemented ， nothing has been changed. the number of cancellederror still remains same. 

The error will cause the saving to the redis failed , while errors accumulated in certain amount , all the operations to redis will be responsed "error" . 

It suffers. and we hope to have a good solution to cater it to get rid of all the cancellederror.

## Expected behaviour

No cancellederror occurs.
or Cancellederror will not impact the application status . 

## Actual behaviour

Too many cancellederror lead the redis malfunction. 

## Steps to reproduce

Sorry, This problem only occurs in live environment , while we set up test environment on localhost or test server , it's hard to reproduce the same error.

## Your environment
Server Side:
centos 7.2 (OS)
python3.6.2 
aiohttp 2.3.0 
aioredis0.33
aiomysql0.0.9
sqlachemy1.1.12
python-socketio (last version)

Client Side：
socketio.js 1.2

issue_title: asyncio.TimeOut downloading large files
issue_body:
## Long story short

Long downloads (>5GB) timeout with asyncio.TimeOutError. 
concurrent.futures._base.TimeoutError

## Expected behaviour

Downloading arbitrarily large downloads should work.

## Actual behaviour
Fails ~5GB in with asyncio.TimeOut

## Steps to reproduce

Try downloading a large file (> 5GB)


```
@asyncio.coroutine
def download_file(url, buffer_size=16144):
    resp = yield from session.get(url)
    while True:
        chunk = yield from resp.content.read(buffer_size)
        if not chunk:
            break
    resp.close()
```
## Your environment
```
shell> pip3 list
aioes (0.7.2)
aiohttp (2.2.5)
asn1crypto (0.22.0)
async-timeout (1.3.0)
asyncio (3.4.3)
asyncssh (1.8.1)
basicauth (0.4.1)
cffi (1.10.0)
chardet (3.0.4)
cryptography (2.0.2)
idna (2.0)
multidict (3.1.3)
pexpect (4.0.1)
pip (8.1.1)
ply (3.10)
ptyprocess (0.5.1)
py (1.4.34)
pyasn1 (0.2.3)
pycparser (2.18)
pycryptodome (3.4.6)
pysmi (0.1.3)
pysnmp (4.3.9)
pytest (3.2.1)
pytest-asyncio (0.6.0)
pytest-repeat (0.3.0)
python-dateutil (2.4.2)
requests (2.10.0)
setuptools (20.7.0)
six (1.10.0)
snimpy (0.8.10)
wheel (0.29.0)
yarl (0.12.0)
```
## Workaround
Use the requests library instead
```
@asyncio.coroutine
def download_file(url, buffer_size=16144):
    resp = requests.get(url, stream=True)
    for chunk in resp.iter_content(chunk_size=buffer_size):
        continue
    resp.close()
```

issue_title: Drop await aiohttp.request(...)
issue_body:
Closing request/session is too error-prone in the case, especially if an exception was generated.

The only robust solution is prohibiting `await request()` syntax but supporting `async with request() as resp:` only.

After implementing proposed restriction we'll get very clean context and behavior.

issue_title: FileResponse sends empty chunked body when returning 304
issue_body:
## Long story short

`FileResponse` (and by extension, `add_static`) may return `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` and the empty chunked body when returning a 304 Not Modified, in violation of [RFC 2616 §10.3.5](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html#sec10.3.5), which states: "The 304 response MUST NOT contain a message-body, and thus is always terminated by the first empty line after the header fields."

## Expected behaviour

A 304 response with no body.

## Actual behaviour

A 304 response with `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` and a 1-byte body (`0`).

## Steps to reproduce
1. Run this with an empty `index.html` in the same directory:
```
from aiohttp import web
from pathlib import Path

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_static('/', Path(__file__).parent)
web.run_app(app, port=8080)
```

2. Send an HTTP/1.1 request with an `If-Modified-Since` that triggers a 304 Not Modified, e.g.
```
# nc localhost 8080
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
If-Modified-Since: Fri, 28 Jul 2017 18:42:00 GMT

```

The response will be something like:

```
HTTP/1.1 304 Not Modified
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Content-Type: application/octet-stream
Date: Fri, 28 Jul 2017 18:42:34 GMT
Server: Python/3.6 aiohttp/2.2.3

0

```

## Your environment

This is using aiohttp 2.2.3, the newest version at this time.

I'm starting up a jupyter notebook server on an AWS instance (Redhat Linux server) to connect to over https. In the config file I have that this should be on port 9999. However when I stop and restart the process with Ctrl-C, the port is not being released, as shown below.

issue_title: explicit override support from plugins?
issue_body:
One of the issues that might crop up as the `override` story gets fleshed out, is that overriding isn't aware of VCS metadata. So for example, the `hg` plugin knows to produce a file tree that doesn't include `.hg`, but an override tree from a local hg clone is going to include that dir.\* That could cause conflicts if multiple overridden modules are imported into the same path (as mentioned in #75).

Would it be worth handling this in some smarter way? The heavy-handed approach would be to have plugins (optionally) produce the override tree in a way that makes sense to them. (The `git` plugin could omit ignored files, for example?) Or, we could have something in `peru.yaml`. Note that both these ideas assume that an override will always be using the same VCS as the module it comes from. Would we want some kind of VCS detection beyond just the original module's type? This is all sounding *much* more complicated than its worth, but I wanted to write it down at least.

\* Note that `git` circumvents this issue because git trees will refuse to represent a `.git` dir.

issue_title: Does this library provide Last.fm callback support?
issue_body:
I am using Last.fm to authenticate users in a Django application and expect Last.fm to send my application a [callback with a temporary authentication token](http://www.last.fm/api/webauth). I am unclear how to do this with `pylast`. The closest API appears to be [SessionKeyGenerator. get_web_auth_session_key(url)](https://github.com/pylast/pylast/blob/7b3ab250df752caeba712b48be1e760bfdbc9983/pylast/__init__.py#L1307), but that doesn't actually extract the token from the callback URL.

I ended up doing it manually using `_Request`:

```python
token = request.GET.get('token')  # Django request  object

network = pylast.LastFMNetwork(
    api_key=LASTFM_API_KEY,
    api_secret=LASTFM_API_SECRET,
)

call = pylast._Request(network, 'auth.getSession', {'token': token})
call.sign_it()

try:
    xml = call.execute()
except pylast.WSError as exc:
    log.error(exc)
    return None
else:
    return xml
```

Did I miss an API that provides similar functionality? Would you consider a PR that provides this functionality?

issue_title: Method _poll_task needs to check if task has started running
issue_body:
Several of our automated tests have show that synchronization and publishing tasks are being polled only once and immediately failing with a `TaskFailedError` exception.

The problem seems to be related to how `_poll_task` only polls a task if its state is `running`. If the state is **not** `running` then we check for the final result, and if `result != 'success' we raise the exception.

Turns out that we have some tasks triggered by the automation that sit on the `planned` state for a while and since `'planned' != 'running'` we get out of the loop and stop polling right after the first check.

Based on the [source code](https://github.com/Dynflow/dynflow/blob/e9c02b60b26e4a8636c85b5d51192d468430d2f7/lib/dynflow/execution_plan.rb#L23) from *TheForeman*, a task can go through the following state transitions:

```ruby
    def self.state_transitions
      @state_transitions ||= { pending:  [:stopped, :scheduled, :planning],
                               scheduled: [:planning, :stopped],
                               planning: [:planned, :stopped],
                               planned:  [:running],
                               running:  [:paused, :stopped],
                               paused:   [:running],
                               stopped:  [] }
    end
```

So I believe that we need to make sure that when polling a task the following states should be observed before either exiting the loop or timing out: `scheduled`, `planning`, `planned` and `running`

An example of a failed test shows (snip):

```json
{"id":"97caeb65-60f1-44df-b482-1d244bb3412d",
"label":"Actions::Katello::Repository::Sync",
"pending":true,
"username":"admin",
"started_at":"2015-09-21T00:56:32Z",
"ended_at":null,
"state":"planned",
"result":"pending",
"progress":0.0,
"input":{"repository":{
"id":9,
"name":"쒲10𣊈쾹10𣊈𩶴麣10𣊈𩶴𨃉䧠惋10𣊈𩶴𨃉𧵷10𣊈𩶴𨃉𧵷𪥚10𣊈𩶴𨃉𧵷𪥚𪚑쾚낕",
"label":"4b6fa137-86c7-427b-b56f-d6078d03c69a"},
"product":{"id":3,"name":"10𣊈𩶴𨃉𧵷𪥚𪚑𩙉䧊10𣊈𩶴𨃉𧵷𪥚𪚑𩙉𩏞",
"label":"7eb07dfd-3dde-4c0d-92a5-ee10619929cb","cp_id":"1442796989475"}
```

issue_title: Incompatibility with fish shell.
issue_body:
When using the fish shell on the remote host, sshuttle doesn't work without adding '--python python' to the commandline.  This is due to the '||' in pycmd in ssh.py.
The error is:
```
Unsupported use of '||'. In fish, please use 'COMMAND; or COMMAND'.
fish: P=python3.5; $P -V 2>/dev/null || P=python; exec "$P" -c 'import sys; verbosity=0; stdin=getattr(sys.stdin,"buffer",sys.stdin); exec(compile(stdin.read(915), "assembler.py", "exec"))'
                                      ^
client: fatal: expected server init string 'SSHUTTLE0001'; got ''
```

This is because fish is not fully POSIX compliant and ssh.py makes assumptions about the shell rather than explicitly calling /bin/sh to ensure compatibility.
Here is a patch that works for me:
```
--- ssh.py.orig	2015-12-14 17:36:49.000000000 -0800
+++ ssh.py	2016-03-08 15:08:16.000000000 -0800
@@ -108,8 +108,8 @@
         if python:
             pycmd = "'%s' -c '%s'" % (python, pyscript)
         else:
-            pycmd = ("P=python3.5; $P -V 2>/dev/null || P=python; "
-                     "exec \"$P\" -c '%s'") % pyscript
+            pycmd = ("/bin/sh -c \'P=python3.5; $P -V 2>/dev/null || P=python; "
+                     "exec \"$P\" -c \\'%s\\'\'") % pyscript
         argv = (sshl +
                 portl +
                 [rhost, '--', pycmd])
```

issue_title: Colon character in file/directory on NFS share causing exception
issue_body:
# Colon character in file/directory on NFS share causing exception

Some characters are illegal on Windows file system but are perfectly fine on third party file systems. Windows Explorer and CMD prompt have no problems with directory called "Foo:bar", but .NET Core (and .NET 4) generates System.NotSupportedException: The given path's format is not supported.

# General

Create a share to a Linux machine or NAS device (tested with Synology NAS and NFS, on Windows Server install Client for NFS)
Map that share to x:\
In the share create a folder Test. Inside the folder Test create a folder 'Foo:bar', note that this is easiest done natively on the device, either web interface, putty or FTP.

Create a .NET Core console app. 

var info = new DirectoryInfo("x:\test")
var dirs = info.GetDirectories();
//dirs contains now one entry of "Foo:bar"
var name = dirs[0].Name // <== already wrong, name is reported as "bar"
dirs = dirs[0].GetDirectories(); // <== NotSupportedException

In code there is likely a check whether the colon is used anywhere else besides for drive letter and generates an exception. Better approach would be that creating new folders and files keeps this check, but for existing entries it should just work just like in File Explorer and CMD prompt, where you can browse inside the directory without any issues.

issue_title: Extension module must be linked with librt on Linux
issue_body:
While trying out vmprof (git clone; python setup.py develop), I've ran into this (Ubuntu 12.04 x86_64):
```
% python -c 'import vmprof'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "vmprof/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
    import _vmprof
ImportError: ./_vmprof.so: undefined symbol: clock_gettime
```
...which is due to `clock_gettime` being defined in librt.so

A simple patch
```diff
--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -20,7 +20,7 @@ else:
                                'src/vmprof.c',
                                ],
                             extra_compile_args=['-Wno-unused'],
-                            libraries=['elf', 'unwind'],
+                            libraries=['elf', 'unwind', 'rt'],
                             extra_link_args=['%s' % libdwarf])]
```
would fix the issue, though not in a portable way (at least some BSDs have this function defined in libc).

Not a pull request as I haven't found a clean&reliable way to do this yet (autotools folks [just test it](http://trac.mpich.org/projects/mpich/ticket/1013), but distutils does not seem to be flexible enough).

issue_title: Provide access to RDS
issue_body:
RDS instances, when they are not public, can only be accessed from certain machines, for example, from the Odd Host. The solution is an SSH tunnel:

```
$ ssh -N -L 45432:<rds_instance_url>:<rds_port> <username>@odd.example.com
```

This makes  the RDS port be available as 45432 on the local machine.
But in order to get access to the Odd Host itself, one has to request access to some other Taupage-based machine, like:

```
$ piu <ip_address> "need to access RDS"
```

This works, but having to refer to an irrelevant Taupage-based service seems redundant.

It would be nice to have the following syntax:

```
$ piu --db <port> "need to access RDS"
```

that will just provision user's public key to the Odd Host only and print a command line for `ssh -N -L ...`

issue_title: environment-{{Arguments.EnvType==sandbox}}
issue_body:
Disclaimer note: this could also be solved through #100 

Would you be willing to accept a PR to add:

```yaml
TaupageConfig:
  environment-{{Arguments.EnvType==sandbox}}:
    DATABASE_HOST: "sql.sandbox.com"
  environment-{{Arguments.EnvType==live}}:
    DATABASE_HOST: "sql.production.com"
```

Or perhaps even shorter:

```yaml
TaupageConfig:
  environment-sandbox:
    DATABASE_HOST: "sql.sandbox.com"
  environment-live:
    DATABASE_HOST: "sql.production.com"
```

i.e. to avoid:

- context switching (managing more than 1 senza yaml file for the same app)
- command line arguments overload when dealing with senza.yaml variations, 
i.e. having to pass a very long list of arguments to senza create in the end resulting into `Template format error: 'Description' length is greater than 1024` particularly when using aws:kms
- having to add EnvType dependant varations logic outside of the senza.yaml file in the app itself or the docker entry point.

Additional note: I wouldn't limit this to `TaupageConfig:` => `environment` but to any senza key value.

issue_title: infinite loop making model for recursive datatype
issue_body:
If I give Z3 a recursive data type and a condition that is unsatisfiable for any finite instance, Z3 will go into an infinite loop during model creation, eventually timing out.  The loop happens because it is trying all possible finite values, of which there are an infinite number of course.  I would like Z3 to instead quickly stop and say "unknown".

I have a candidate fix for this, but it causes three tests in z3test to fail, so I know it isn't completely right.

My reduced input example is attached as rdp-min.smt2.txt:

[rdp-min.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/239664/rdp-min.smt2.txt)

When I run Z3 version 4.4.1 like so:

```
  $ z3 -t:2000 rdp-min.smt2
  unknown
```
it runs for about 2 seconds and reports "unknown".

When I add some tracing:

```
  $ z3 -t:2000 -v:100 -tr:datatype rdp-min.smt2
  [...]
  (smt.final-check "datatype")
  (smt.stats :time 2.01 :before-memory 1.72 :after-memory 2.00)
  (tactic-exception "smt tactic failed to show goal to be sat/unsat")
  unknown
```
the timeout is clear, and near the end of .z3-trace, there is:

```
  -------- [datatype] internalize_term ../src/smt/theory_datatype.cpp:231 ---------
  internalizing term:
  (let ((a!1 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf theList))))))
  (let ((a!2 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!1))))))
  (let ((a!3 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!2))))))
  (let ((a!4 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!3))))))
  (let ((a!5 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!4))))))
  (let ((a!6 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!5))))))
  (let ((a!7 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!6))))))
  (let ((a!8 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!7))))))
  (let ((a!9 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!8))))))
  (let ((a!10 (restOf (restOf (restOf (restOf a!9))))))
    (is-End (restOf (restOf (restOf a!10))))))))))))))
  ------------------------------------------------
```
The above term has 43 "restOf" applications; if I let it run for 20s instead, it times out after building a list with length of about 140.

Stepping through the code in gdb, what's happening seems fairly straightforward.  The prover core first thinks the formula is satisfiable, and asks each theory to help build a model.  The "datatype" theory sees a term with indeterminate constructor, "(restOf theList)", and forces a case split on its constructor.  The "End" case is quickly rejected, but the "Elem" case creates "(restOf (restOf theList))", which again has indeterminate constructor.  When the attempt to build a model resumes, the process repeats, building longer and longer lists, until Z3 times out.

I have a candidate fix, attached as z3-rdp-changes.txt:

[z3-rdp-changes.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/239666/z3-rdp-changes.txt)

With these changes, my reduced test case works:

```
  $ z3 -t:2000 -v:100 -tr:datatype rdp-min.smt2
  [...]
  (smt.stats :time 0.00 :before-memory 1.72 :after-memory 1.72)
  (tactic-exception "smt tactic failed to show goal to be sat/unsat")
  unknown
```
However, these tests in z3test fail:

```
  regressions/smt2/z3.17.smt2
  regressions/smt2/mut_rec4sdk_output1.smt2
    expected "sat", got "unknown"

  regressions/smt2/z3.16.smt2
    expected "sat, unsat", got "unknown, unknown"
```
It would seem that I need to allow Z3 to do bounded unfolding of recursive data types, but I don't know how, so I'd appreciate feedback on whether this is a good approach, and if so, suggestions on how to add an unfolding limit.

issue_title: Bad performance using datatype discriminators
issue_body:
Here's a small example where using `ite` and a `is-`-discriminator destroys the performance of z3:

    (declare-sort sk_x 0)
    (declare-datatypes ()
      ((list (nil) (cons (head sk_x) (tail list)))))
    (declare-const z list)
    (declare-const w list)
    (declare-fun append (list list) list)
    (assert
      (forall ((x list) (y list))
        (= (append x y)
          (ite (is-cons x) (cons (head x) (append (tail x) y)) y))))
    (assert
      (distinct (append nil (append z w)) (append (append nil z) w)))
    (check-sat)

This does not terminate on 4.3.2, 4.3.1 or the git version. However, defining
append in this way by pattern-matching gives an almost instant `unsat`:

    (declare-sort sk_x 0)
    (declare-datatypes ()
      ((list (nil) (cons (head sk_x) (tail list)))))
    (declare-const z list)
    (declare-const w list)
    (declare-fun append (list list) list)
    (assert (forall ((ys list)) (= (append nil ys) ys)))
    (assert
      (forall ((x sk_x) (xs list) (ys list))
        (= (append (cons x xs) ys) (cons x (append xs ys)))))
    (assert
      (distinct (append nil (append z w)) (append (append nil z) w)))
    (check-sat)

Thanks,
Dan

issue_body:
Hi,

This is somewhat new: I'm getting an answer which is actually incorrect for the optimization query.
The associated behavior is also very strange.
Here is my minimal reproducible example:

```
(declare-fun |main::i@3| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::i@4| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::source@2| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::nodecount@3| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::edgecount@3| () Int)

(assert
    (and 
       (and (= |main::i@4| (+ 1 |main::i@3|)))
       (<= |main::nodecount@3| 4)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) |main::edgecount@3|) 19)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::nodecount@3|) 0)
       (<= |main::edgecount@3| 19)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::edgecount@3|) 0)
       (<= (+ |main::i@3| (* (- 1) |main::nodecount@3|)) 0)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) |main::nodecount@3|) 4)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) |main::source@2|) 0)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) 0)
       (<= (+ |main::i@3| (* (- 1) |main::source@2|)) 1)
       (<= |main::source@2| 0)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::source@2|) 0)
       (<= |main::source@2| 0)
))
(maximize (* (- 1) |main::i@4|))
(set-option :opt.optsmt_engine basic)
(set-option :opt.priority box)
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
Z3 gives me the answer "-18", and the model `i@3 = -19`.
Yet the constraint line clearly says: `(<= (* (- 1) |main::i@3|) 0)` (`i@3` has to be positive).

Moreover, the query exhibits many strange behaviors. For one, plugging in back model values as assertions expectedly gives "UNSAT" (by chance, would it be possible to have a "sanity-check-debug-mode" for optimization which would actually check that the obtained bound is feasible?)
Moreover, replacing the assertions `source@2 <= 0 /\ -source@2 <= 0` with `source@2 = 0` (which is the same), changes the answer to -1.

issue_title: How to get the gradient( dL/dw) of the first hiden layer during training?
More flexible DenseLayer
issue_body:
A common use case for a dense layer is to stack it on top of a recurrent layer to compute a prediction at every time step. Since the recurrent layer output is of shape `(batchsize, seq_len, feats)`, this requires three layers: A `ReshapeLayer(..., (-1, feats))`, the `DenseLayer(..., num_units)`, and possibly another `ReshapeLayer(..., (batchsize, seq_len, num_units))`. The latter requires either the `batchsize` or `seq_len` to be known at compile time, or obtained as symbolic variables.

The operation inside the `DenseLayer` is quite simple: It consists of `T.flatten()` followed by `T.dot()`.
Now `T.dot(x, y)` already supports arbitrary dimensionality for `x`, performing the dot product over the last axis only. The only reason we cannot directly use it on the output of a recurrent layer is that it flattens the input to be a matrix, in this case of shape `(batchsize, seq_len * feats)`.

We could make the `DenseLayer` much more flexible simply by allowing to specify the number of axes to be flattened (or to be kept). I propose to add a keyword argument with the following semantics:
* If it's a positive integer, this gives the number of axes not to be flattened. E.g., setting it to `1` (the default) means to keep the first axis and flatten the remaining ones. Put differently, it gives the index of the axis all following ones are to be collapsed under.
* If it's a negative integer, it also gives the index of the axis all following ones are to be collapsed under, counted from the end as is customary in Python for negative indices. E.g., setting it to `-2` means to flatten the last two axes, and `-1` means to only flatten the last axis, which does nothing.
* If it's zero, we throw an error because we think this is never intended and based on a misunderstanding? Otherwise, this would flatten all the input into a single vector, losing the batch dimension on the way.

I'm not too sure about the name of the keyword argument, though. It could be `keep_axes`, or `distribute_over` (as the dot product is distributed over the given number of axes).
Alternatively, we could also have a full-blown `shared_axes` argument listing the axes to distribute the dot product over, but this would involve moving all the others to the end before the dot product and restoring the order afterwards (e.g., a `(3,4,5,6)` tensor with `shared_axes=(0,-1)` would have a `(20,num_units)` weight matrix and produce a `(3,num_units,6)` output) -- maybe that's too much flexibility.

admin_tools: (admin_tools.W001) You must add "admin_tools.template_loaders.TemplateLoader" in your template loaders variable, see: https://django-admin-tools.readthedocs.org/en/latest/configuration.html
```

When added as instructed:

```
TEMPLATES = [
    {
        'BACKEND': 'django.template.backends.django.DjangoTemplates',
        'DIRS': [  
		...
        ],
        'APP_DIRS': False,
        'OPTIONS': {
            'context_processors': [
		...
            ],
            'loaders': [
                'admin_tools.template_loaders.TemplateLoader',
```

the following error is returned:

```
  File "PATH/TO/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/utils/module_loading.py", line 29, in import_string
    return getattr(module, class_name)
ImportError: Module "django.template.loaders.app_directories" does not define a "TemplateLoader" attribute/class
```

I noticed that ```admin_tools.template_loaders``` has a class named ```Loader``` as seen:

https://github.com/django-admin-tools/django-admin-tools/blob/master/admin_tools/template_loaders.py#L41

More than happy to issue a PR if that helps, wanted to confirm this minor issue or get corrected in the case I missed something. 

Thanks for all the hard work.

issue_title: ROOT not correctly setup
issue_body:
The ROOT that ganga is trying to use for merging is not usable the way ganga tries to use it, as it requires a newer `libstdc++` than available on the system. A LHCb-specific solution could be to give the possibility to configure the entire prefix and set it to `lb-run root ` for LHCb. I'm not sure how version selection can be implemented in this scheme. 

Version: 7.1.3
Tested platforms: `x86_64-slc6-gcc62-opt` and `x86_64-centos7-gcc62-opt`
Reproducer:
```python
import GangaCore
import commands
cmd = GangaCore.Utility.root.getrootprefix()[1] + 'hadd'
commands.getstatusoutput(cmd)

# /cvmfs/lhcb.cern.ch/lib/RootConfig/pro/x86_64-centos7-gcc62-opt/bin/hadd: /lib64/libstdc++.so.6: version `GLIBCXX_3.4.21' not found (required by /cvmfs/lhcb.cern.ch/lib/RootConfig/pro/x86_64-centos7-gcc62-opt/bin/hadd)
# /cvmfs/lhcb.cern.ch/lib/RootConfig/pro/x86_64-centos7-gcc62-opt/bin/hadd: /lib64/libstdc++.so.6: version `CXXABI_1.3.8' not found (required by /cvmfs/lhcb.cern.ch/lib/RootConfig/pro/x86_64-centos7-gcc62-opt/lib/libGraf3d.so)
# ...
```

PS It might be useful to run this or something similar to see where libraries come from.
```sh
lb-run root bash -c 'ldd `which hadd`'
```

issue_title: Update orphan delete function
issue_body: The current orphan deletion function makes an HTTP POST call. [The spec](https://docs.pulpproject.org/en/2.14/dev-guide/integration/rest-api/content/orphan.html#remove-all-orphaned-content) says that an HTTP DELETE call should be made. Update the orphan deletion function so that it complies with the spec.

issue_title: LazyFrame in Atari wrappers
issue_body:
I notice in the comment [here](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/common/atari_wrappers_deprecated.py#L160): "you won't believe how complex the previous solution was". 

I don't understand why it is complicated. The LazyFrame can be implemented simply as an object that holds a `list` of last _n_ frames. During training, we can call a method to concatenate the list into a numpy array. Because the `list` stores references, not copies, no memory is wasted. Overloading `__array__` seems nothing more than a syntactic sugar that enables `np.array(lazy_frame)` syntax.

Or am I missing something? Thanks!

issue_title: Rework `user_formulas` to support a map of `{formula_name}: {formula_source}`
issue_body:
Right now, effectively, we use `formula_termination_strings` to determine the formula name (and thus the name of the formula directory in the <salt_srv> root). That gets to be rather hacky. Here's the current config definition:

```
      formula_termination_strings:
        - -master
        - -latest
      user_formulas:
        #To add other formulas, make sure it is a url to a zipped file as follows:
        #- https://s3.amazonaws.com/salt-formulas/systemprep-formula-master.zip
        #- https://s3.amazonaws.com/salt-formulas/ash-linux-formula-master.zip
        #To "overwrite" submodule formulas, make sure name matches submodule names.
```

 Instead, change the spec for `user_formulas` to be a list of maps of the formula name and the formula source:

```
      user_formulas:
        #- systemprep-formula: https://s3.amazonaws.com/salt-formulas/systemprep-formula-master.zip
        #- ash-linux-formula: https://s3.amazonaws.com/salt-formulas/ash-linux-formula-master.zip
```

This would deprecate `formula_termination_strings`.

The reason seems to be some zero-byte padding that happens in protobuf serialization to the underlying markdown library. The suggested fix works for us but we are unsure if this is the proper way to solve it? 

```
import tensorflow as tf
sess = tf.InteractiveSession()
text = """# Heading
\tPreformatted text,

summary_op = tf.summary.text('text', tf.convert_to_tensor(text))
summary_writer = tf.summary.FileWriter('/tmp/tensorboard', sess.graph)
text = sess.run(summary_op)
summary_writer.add_summary(text, 0)
summary_writer.flush()
summary_writer.close()
```

```
def markdown_and_sanitize(markdown_string):

  It uses the table extension; while that's not a part of standard

  Returns:

  """
  # Convert to utf-8 whenever we have a binary input.
  if isinstance(markdown_string, six.binary_type):

  markdown_string = markdown_string.replace('\x00', '')

issue_title: Feature request: give new parameter to a tf.summary.scalar to indicate which graph it belongs to
issue_body:
I have stumbled upon the difficulties of plotting more plots into the same graph using tf.summary.scalar during one session run. I, like many others, want to plot a train and validation curve on the same graph, but have only seen workarounds to this, where two different filewriters create two different directories for saving the two different plots. This is indeed a workaround, and sometimes it does not even work - for example when I want to save a tf.summary.scalar with the same name for train and validation set during one run. Another example that this workaround doesn't work on, as I saw someone else point out in issue #300 , is the A3C algorithm.

I have read this thread and seen that there is a tensorboard plugin (tensorboard/plugins/custom_scalar) that also is a workaround to the issue; a plugin that I, unfortunately, haven't managed to get to work properly. I wanted to mention this to show that I am aware that this plugin exists, and to point out that I am looking for a different solution.

The solution I want is to make it possible to provide a parameter to tf.summary.scalar to indicate which graph it belongs to. The tf.summary.scalar would then have both a unique identifier in the name parameter, along with a graph identifier in the graph_name parameter. The complete function would then look like this:

tf.summary.scalar(name, tensor, graph_name, collections, family)

Is this possible?

issue_title: Append on Visdom.line expect different data format
issue_body:
Thanks again for the great library.
I was trying the python example code, and it seems to me there's a problem with the input data format in the following block:
```
# line updates
win = viz.line(
    X=np.column_stack((np.arange(0, 10), np.arange(0, 10))),
    Y=np.column_stack((np.linspace(5, 10, 10), np.linspace(5, 10, 10) + 5)),
)
viz.line(
    X=np.column_stack((np.arange(10, 20), np.arange(10, 20))),
    Y=np.column_stack((np.linspace(5, 10, 10), np.linspace(5, 10, 10))),
    win=win,
    update='append'
)
```
When creating the line plot, the two series of data are stacked as columns, such as every Nth row of X contains Nth value of all the series.
But using the same format for updating the plot (with `update='append') doesn't work. The plot doesn't get updated as expected. 
It is necessary to stack the two series as rows using something like below (or by stacking them in columns then transposing them):
```
viz.line(
    X=np.row_stack((np.arange(10, 20), np.arange(10, 20))),
    Y=np.row_stack((np.linspace(5, 10, 10), np.linspace(5, 10, 10))),
    win=win,
    update='append'
)
```
Doing this updates each series by appending the new points as expected. 

I think the reason is that when creating a line plot initially, the following line ravels the data series in Fortran-style order:
[https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/__init__.py#L453](url)

issue_title: Date must be preserved as text
issue_body:
I am using *csvsql 1.0.2* with something as
```sh
cat myDataset.csv  | \
csvsql --insert --tables t --db  postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/sandbox   
```
where `myDataset.csv` have column `c1` with non-ISO dates, that  must be interpreted as strings,
```
c1,c2
03/02/2018 10:21:42,etc.
03/02/2018 18:03:52,etc.
05/02/2018 20:11:20,etc.
```
 but  `csvsql`  enforced  c01 to be interpreted as *timestamp without time zone*. A big mistake, generating future dates,
```
select c1 from quest1resps ;
    c1    
---------------------
 2018-03-02 10:21:42
 2018-03-02 18:03:52
 2018-05-02 20:11:20
 ```
It is not reliable. Any "magic" interpretation must be added only when an option is flagged: **default behaviour must be the most reliable**, not the "most magic".

issue_title: Keep pandas DataFrame attributes
issue_body:
it's gr8 when sampling method handle `pandas` data as it is, like `sklearn.cross_validation.train_test_split` does. Currently, it coerces to `np.ndarray`.

```
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from imblearn.combine import SMOTEENN

# Generate the dataset
X = pd.DataFrame(np.random.randn(100, 5), columns=list('ABCDE'))
y = pd.Series(np.array([0, 1]).repeat([80, 20]))

sm = SMOTEENN()
X_resampled, y_resampled = sm.fit_sample(X, y)
type(X_resampled), type(y_resampled)
# (numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray)
```

`train_test_split` can keep `pandas` types.

```
from sklearn.cross_validation import train_test_split
X_train, y_train, X_test, y_test = train_test_split(X, y)
type(X_train), type(y_train), type(X_test), type(y_test)
# (pandas.core.frame.DataFrame,
#  pandas.core.frame.DataFrame,
#  pandas.core.series.Series,
#  pandas.core.series.Series)
```

If acceptable, I'll submit a PR.

issue_title: PyCapsule_New calls have no destructor - memory leak
issue_body:
None of the calls to `PyCapsule_New` are passing in a destructor. The result is that the various heap-allocated chunks inside these capsules will eventually leak.

For example, calling `lz4.frame.makePrefs()` in a loop will eventually consume all memory even if the result is not bound to a variable.

issue_title: Django ImageField is a SubFactory now, should it be?
issue_body:
I've received an issue https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-factoryboy/issues/58

pytest-factoryboy introspects the declarations in order to create fixtures automatically related to the current factory.

So far I'm comparing isinstance(decl, SubFactory) and isinstance(decl, RelatedFactory).
Now this breaks for the django.ImageField since it is a subclass of a Dict and also a SubFactory (really weird hierarchy). Is it not abused? Should it really be a SubFactory?
If ImageField is a SubFactory why doesn't it accept a factory as a parameter in order to respect the interface of the SubFactory (common OOP sense). If ImageField IS A SubFactory == False then why it won't use composition and invoke a dict factory inside the implementation?

I could check exact types like if type(decl, SubFactory) then create fixture for the model referenced by SubFactory. But this will prevent from subclassing a SubFactory in the valid cases.

Any suggestions?

class Event():

issue_title: Bongacams Streamlink plugin for ffmpeg + ffplay
issue_body:
This plugin starts ffplay and ffmpeg at the same time. Each in separate frame so that you can easily stoped them later. For ffplay I recommend the latest but ffmpeg must be older, 
I recommend this: [https://www.videohelp.com/download/ffmpeg-2.8.3-win32-static.7z](url)
All this is necessary because the recorded file is FLV quality even better than the one recorded with rtmpdump, since it is not necessary fixed after recording. Newer ffmpeg doesn't support the codec I use.

bongacams.py
`import sys, os, re, time, datetime, requests, random, command
from io import BytesIO
from hashlib import md5
from streamlink.compat import bytes, urljoin, urlparse, urlunparse
from streamlink.exceptions import PluginError, NoStreamsError
from streamlink.packages.flashmedia.types import AMF0String, AMF0Value, U32BE
from streamlink.packages.flashmedia import AMFPacket, AMFMessage
from streamlink.plugin.api import validate, http
from streamlink.plugin import Plugin
from streamlink.stream import RTMPStream

CONST_FLASH_VER = "WIN 25,0,0,127"
CONST_DEFAULT_SWF_LOCATION = '/swf/chat/BCamChat.swf?cache=201705030900'
CONST_AMF_GATEWAY_LOCATION = '/tools/amf.php'
CONST_AMF_GATEWAY_PARAM = 'x-country'
CONST_DEFAULT_COUNTRY_CODE = 'en'
CONST_HEADERS = {}
CONST_HEADERS['User-Agent'] = 'Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 6.1) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) '
CONST_HEADERS['User-Agent'] += 'Chrome/54.0.2840.99 Safari/537.36 OPR/41.0.2353.69'

url_re = re.compile(r"(http(s)?://)?(\w{2}.)?(bongacams.com)/([\w\d_-]+)")
swf_re = re.compile(r"/swf/\w+/\w+.swf\?cache=\d+")

amf_msg_schema = validate.Schema({"status": "success","userData": {"username": validate.text},
    "localData":{"NC_ConnUrl": validate.url(scheme="rtmp"),"NC_AccessKey": validate.length(32),
    "dataKey": validate.length(32),},"performerData": {"username": validate.text,}})

class bongacams(Plugin):
    @classmethod
    def can_handle_url(self, url):
        return url_re.match(url)

    def _get_stream_uid(self, username):
        m = md5(username.encode('utf-8') + str(time.time()).encode('utf-8'))
        return m.hexdigest()

    def _get_streams(self):
        match = url_re.match(self.url)

        stream_page_scheme = 'https'
        stream_page_domain = match.group(4)
        stream_page_path = match.group(5)
        print("\n => NAME: {} <=".format(stream_page_path))
        country_code = CONST_DEFAULT_COUNTRY_CODE
        is_paid_show = False

        # create http session and set headers
        http_session = http
        http_session.headers.update(CONST_HEADERS)

        # get swf url and cookies
        r = http_session.get(urlunparse((stream_page_scheme, stream_page_domain, stream_page_path, '', '', '')))

        # redirect to profile page means stream is offline
        if '/profile/' in r.url:
            raise NoStreamsError(self.url)
        if not r.ok:
            self.logger.info("STATUS CODE FOR {0}: {1}\n", r.url, r.status_code)
            raise NoStreamsError(self.url)
        if len(http_session.cookies) == 0:
            raise PluginError("Can't get a cookies")

        if urlparse(r.url).netloc != stream_page_domain:
            # then redirected to regional subdomain
            country_code = urlparse(r.url).netloc.split('.')[0].lower()

        # time to set variables
        baseurl = urlunparse((stream_page_scheme, urlparse(r.url).netloc, '', '', '', ''))
        amf_gateway_url = urljoin(baseurl, CONST_AMF_GATEWAY_LOCATION)
        stream_page_url = urljoin(baseurl, stream_page_path)
        print("\n => URL: {} <=".format(stream_page_url))

        match = swf_re.search(r.text)
        if match:
            swf_url = urljoin(baseurl, match.group())
            print("\n => SWF: {} <=\n".format(swf_url))
        else:
            # most likely it means that country/region banned
            # can try use default swf-url
            swf_url = urljoin(baseurl, CONST_DEFAULT_SWF_LOCATION)
            self.logger.info("SWF URL NOT FOUND. WILL TRY {0}\n", swf_url)

        # create amf query
        amf_message = AMFMessage("svDirectAmf.getRoomData", "/1", [stream_page_path, is_paid_show])
        amf_packet = AMFPacket(version=0)
        amf_packet.messages.append(amf_message)

        # send request and close http-session
        r = http_session.post(url=amf_gateway_url,params={CONST_AMF_GATEWAY_PARAM: country_code},data=bytes(amf_packet.serialize()))
        http_session.close()

        if r.status_code != 200:
            raise PluginError("unexpected status code for {0}: {1}", r.url, r.status_code)

        amf_response = AMFPacket.deserialize(BytesIO(r.content))

        if len(amf_response.messages) != 1 or amf_response.messages[0].target_uri != "/1/onResult":
            raise PluginError("unexpected response from amf gate")

        stream_source_info = amf_msg_schema.validate(amf_response.messages[0].value)
        print(" => SOURCE STREAM INFO: {} <=\n".format(stream_source_info))

        rtmp_params = stream_source_info['localData']['NC_ConnUrl']
        playpath_params = "%s?uid=%s" % (''.join(('stream_', stream_page_path)),self._get_stream_uid(stream_source_info['userData']['username']))
        print(" => RTMP: {} <=\n".format(rtmp_params))
        print(" => PATH: {} <=\n".format(playpath_params))
        url1 = rtmp_params + '/' + playpath_params
        url2 = rtmp_params + '/stream_' + stream_page_path + '?uid=4909728dbf3f28f098bbee20671355e7'
        timestamp = str(time.strftime("%d%m%Y_%H%M%S"))
        path = "C:\Videos\BC\\"
        filename = stream_page_path + '_BC_' + timestamp + '.flv'
        pf = (path + filename)

        command = ('start ffmpeg -i {} -c:v copy -c:a libvo_aacenc -b:a 128k -timeout 600 -rtmp_swfurl {} {}'.format(url1,swf_url,pf))
        os.system(command)
        command = ('start ffplay -i {} -infbuf -autoexit -x 640 -y 480 -rtmp_swfurl {} -window_title "{}"'.format(url2,swf_url,pf))
        os.system(command)

        print(" => Start PLAY + RECORD => " + filename + " <= ")
        print
        raw_input(" => Press Enter to exit . . . ")

__plugin__ = bongacams`

issue_title: Reliance on node identifiers in decanonicalization of complex/composite
issue_body: When decanonicalizing the node representing a complex or composite, the code tries to get information about the latent node tuple, which is bad because this is just a clever way to maintain consistent and unique identifiers. If one chooses to hash these tuples, then this decanonicalization breaks, when it definitely shouldn't.

issue_title: Inaccurate computation of Lon/Lat bounds in `geobox_info`
issue_body:
### Expected behaviour

- For every point `X` 
   - IF `X.lonlat not in ds.extent_lon_lat`, THEN `X.xy_native not in ds.extent_native`

### Actual behaviour

- For some datasets `ds.extent_lon_lat` is computed incorrectly and is smaller then the actual lon/lat bounds, so some points within the dataset footprint will be classified as outside when using `lonlat`

Offending code is here:

https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/d561b4a1c8a8dce4c98fd524eca5899e98303241/datacube/model/utils.py#L47-L62

This just transforms 4 points of a bounding rectangle to EPSG:4362

```python
to_lonlat( bounds(valid_data | image_bounds))
```

this computation sometimes results in smaller bounds than actual, a more proper way to first "increase resolution" of the native polygon, possibly add some buffer, then translate that to EPSG:4326 and then compute envelope of the result. Converting geometry to a different CRS with increased resolution is already supported by `datacube.utils.geometry.Geometry` class, but we should probably use "higher resolution" than what is currently default (100km)

```python
envelope(polygon(valid_data|image_bounds).to_crs(epsg4326, resolution=1000))
```

issue_title: MockClock rate=1.0
issue_body:
https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/1075239f129e735a540ced313c7fbbdbdb859af5/trio/testing/_mock_clock.py#L90

Hi!

I've just found your project and am loving it so far. I am building an automation platform and had implemented this sort of `MockClock` synchronously, so I love what you've done here!

```python
class MockClock(Clock):
    ...
    def __init__(self, rate=0.0, autojump_threshold=inf):
        # when the real clock said 'real_base', the virtual time was
        # 'virtual_base', and since then it's advanced at 'rate' virtual
        # seconds per real second.
        self._real_base = 0.0
        self._virtual_base = 0.0
        self._rate = 1.0
```

Would recommend you use an initial rate of `1.0`, as it then implements a sort of "time travel" feature. Unless of course, you're intentionally wanting to keep the virtual time as dissimilar to [real-time as possible](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-core.html#time-and-clocks). This is super cool and I've heavily based my clock off of your `MockClock`.

This is more of a non-issue as it's about a custom implementation of the clock, but wanted to bring it up and show you another possible use case for Clocks!

Thanks for the cool work, I'm excited to keep exploring.
\- SN

issue_title: GPLv3?
issue_body:
I just realized python-gitlab is license under GPLv3.

GPLv3 license is arguably not suitable for a library. 
For example, I cannot use it in https://github.com/buildbot/buildbot which is GPLv2.
I can only use it for programs in GPLv3 which are very sparse nowadays. In 2015: 8% of projects in github are GPLv3 https://blog.github.com/2015-03-09-open-source-license-usage-on-github-com/

I believe that python-gitlab has good value and is well designed. Please consider putting it in a less restrictive license so that it can be more widely used.
